class.title: Class
class.definition: A blueprint for creating objects. Defines structure (attributes) and behavior (operations).
class.when: Used to model domain entities that have characteristics and actions.
class.example: The `Student` class with attributes `id`, `name`, `enrolledCourses` and methods like `enroll(course)`.
class.iso_rules: ISO 19505: A class must have a unique name within the diagram and can define member visibility.

interface.title: Interface
interface.definition: A contract that defines a set of operations a class must implement.
interface.when: Used to define expected behavior without specifying the implementation.
interface.example: The `PaymentProcessor` interface defines operations like `process(amount)` and `refund(transactionId)`.
interface.iso_rules: ISO 19505: An interface is a special kind of classifier that specifies a set of public operations.

enumeration.title: Enumeration
enumeration.definition: A data type that contains a fixed set of named values.
enumeration.when: Used to model attributes that can only take specific predefined values.
enumeration.iso_rules: ISO 19505: An enumeration is a classifier that defines literals (constant values).

association.title: Association
association.definition: A relationship between two classes indicating one can interact with or use the other.
association.when: Used to model general relationships between entities (e.g., 'Student enrolls in Course').
association.example: A bidirectional association between `Student` and `Course` with multiplicity `*` to `*`.
association.iso_rules: ISO 19505: An association connects two or more classifiers. It includes name, direction, and multiplicity.

inheritance.title: Generalization (Inheritance)
inheritance.definition: An 'is-a' relationship where a specialized class inherits structure and behavior from a general class.
inheritance.when: Used to model class hierarchies where one class is more specific than another.
inheritance.example: `Dog` and `Cat` inherit from `Animal`. They share attributes like `age`, `name` but have specific operations.
inheritance.iso_rules: ISO 19505: Generalization is a specialization/generalization relationship between classifiers.

composition.title: Composition
composition.definition: A strong 'has-a' relationship where the part cannot exist without the whole.
composition.when: Used when there is strong ownership: the container is responsible for the lifecycle of its parts.
composition.example: `Company` composes `Departments`. If the company is deleted, its departments are too.
composition.iso_rules: ISO 19505: Composition is a strong aggregation represented with a filled diamond.

aggregation.title: Aggregation
aggregation.definition: A weak 'has-a' relationship where the part can exist without the whole.
aggregation.when: Used when there is weak ownership: the container simply groups the parts.
aggregation.example: `Team` aggregates `Players`. A player can exist without a team.
aggregation.iso_rules: ISO 19505: Aggregation is a special relationship with multiplicity where parts retain independence.

dependency.title: Dependency
dependency.definition: A unidirectional usage relationship where one class depends on another to function.
dependency.when: Used when a class needs another without ownership (e.g., passing it as a parameter).
dependency.iso_rules: ISO 19505: Dependency is a unidirectional relationship indicating temporal coupling.

realization.title: Realization (Implementation)
realization.definition: A relationship where a concrete class implements the operations defined by an interface.
realization.when: Used to show which operations of an interface are implemented by a class.
realization.iso_rules: ISO 19505: Realization specifies that a classifier implements a contract defined by an interface.

note.title: Note
note.definition: An annotation element that provides additional information, comments, or constraints.
note.when: Used to clarify business rules, constraints, or details that do not fit in the diagram.
note.example: A note attached to a class: 'Email must be unique in the system'.
note.iso_rules: ISO 19505: A note is a graphical symbol containing freely formatted comments.

export_luml.title: Export to .luml
export_luml.description: Save your diagram in LibreUML format (compressed JSON).
how_to: The file will download with all diagram information
export_luml.tip: Use this format to share diagrams between LibreUML users and versions.

export_java.title: Export to Java Code
export_java.description: Convert your UML diagram to ready-to-use Java code.
export_java.tip: Generated code includes classes, interfaces, inheritance, and Javadoc annotations.

export_images.title: Export to Images (PNG/SVG)
export_images.description: Convert your diagram to PNG image or scalable SVG.
how_to: Download the image ready for presentations
export_images.tip: SVG is scalable without quality loss. PNG is ideal for Word/PowerPoint.

export_xmi.title: Export to XMI (ISO 19505)
export_xmi.description: Export to XMI (XML Metadata Interchange), the OMG standard for interoperability.
how_to: XMI is compatible with Enterprise Architect, Astah, ArchiMate
export_xmi.tip: Make sure the concepts used comply with ISO 19505 for maximum compatibility.

projects_vs_standalone.title: Projects vs. Visitor Files
projects_vs_standalone.description: Understand the difference between a Project (multiple diagrams) and a Visitor File (single diagram).
how_to: **Project:** Click 'New Project' → place multiple diagrams in one folder
**Visitor File:** Click 'Open File' → opens a .luml without a project
In a Project: changes are automatically saved in the project folder
In Visitor mode: changes are saved in the single file when pressing Ctrl+S
projects_vs_standalone.tip: Use Projects for large work (thesis, systems). Use Visitor files for quick examples.

autosave.title: Auto-save (Ctrl+S)
autosave.description: LibreUML saves your work automatically when you press Ctrl+S.
how_to: You will see a 'Saved' notification in the bottom corner
Your work is safe on the file system
autosave.tip: Ctrl+S saves immediately. You can also use File → Save.
